region_of_origin: Taiwan (popularised across Southeast Asia and Singapore)

ontology.foodex2_description: Tea-based beverage with tapioca pearls (bubble tea)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for milk-free flavoured bubble tea; A0FKM (tea-based beverage with tapioca pearls) used as closest parent. Milk-free and flavoured attributes are specified in the food name and description.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured bubble tea without milk; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: blended with flavoured syrup or fruit concentrate
served with tapioca pearls or optional toppings

serving.description: 1 medium cup (approximately 500 ml / 500 g as served)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of flavoured bubble tea (no milk) is one medium takeaway cup of approximately 500 ml, equivalent to roughly 500 g, as commonly sold at bubble tea outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Bubble Tea (No Milk) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Flavoured bubble tea no milk' (cr_id D08010023). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sugar-Sweetened Beverages and Fruit-Flavoured Tea Drinks — analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for flavoured bubble tea without milk. GI of 70 is estimated by analogy to sucrose-sweetened fruit-flavoured beverages (GI ~65–72) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. The beverage contains 9.54 g sugar/100 g (predominantly sucrose and fructose from flavoured syrup) with negligible fibre (0.03 g/100 g), supporting a high GI classification. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (70 × 12.44 × 5) / 100 = 43.5 g, confirming very high glycemic impact per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.54 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage; AHA evidence links SSB consumption to insulin-driven sodium retention and elevated blood pressure independent of dietary sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit flavoured bubble tea (no milk) due to its high sugar load (9.54 g/100 g), which independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention; request 0% sugar level if consumed and avoid tapioca pearls.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.54 g sugar/100 g, GI 70 (high), and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g), causing rapid postprandial glucose spike; ADA SSB restriction applies.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid flavoured bubble tea (no milk) as it is a high-GI (70) sugar-sweetened beverage delivering 9.54 g sugar/100 g with virtually no fibre, producing rapid glucose absorption; if consumed, request 0% sugar and avoid tapioca pearls to minimise glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular sugar-sweetened beverage consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular event risk per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit flavoured bubble tea (no milk) due to its high free-sugar content (9.54 g/100 g per 100 g; ~47.7 g per 500 ml serving), which is linked to hypertriglyceridaemia, reduced HDL, and increased CVD risk; unsweetened tea alternatives are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Bubble Tea (No Milk) is a freshly prepared, fruit or tea-based sweetened beverage popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia, originating from Taiwan in the 1980s. It is made by combining a tea base — typically green tea, black tea, or herbal fruit tea — with flavoured syrups or fruit concentrates and sugar, then shaking or blending with ice. Unlike milk tea variants, this version contains no dairy, making it suitable for lactose-intolerant and vegan consumers. It is commonly served with customisable toppings such as tapioca pearls (boba), grass jelly, aloe vera, or popping boba. The beverage has a light, refreshing, fruity or floral flavour profile with a sweet taste and a characteristic chewy texture from the pearls. Per 100 g, it provides 48 kcal, 12.44 g carbohydrate (of which 9.54 g is sugar), 0.01 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 0.03 g dietary fibre, and 1 mg sodium. The high sugar content (9.54 g/100 g) and high glycemic index (~70) make this a sugar-sweetened beverage of clinical concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. A standard 500 ml serving delivers approximately 47.7 g of free sugar and a glycemic load of ~43.5, far exceeding WHO daily free-sugar recommendations. Clinically, it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and should be consumed with caution or avoided by those with metabolic conditions. Requesting 0% sugar level significantly reduces glycaemic impact.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bubble tea without milk?
Is bubble tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of bubble tea?
Can I drink bubble tea if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of bubble tea?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010023) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sucrose-sweetened fruit beverages, which is the closest defensible approved GI source.
notes: fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D08010023); this is a non-dairy, non-fat fruit/tea-based sweetened beverage with no added fat by formulation — confirmed zero. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this freshly prepared beverage category are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D08010023 or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent product as of 2026. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened fruit-flavoured beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI entry exists for this specific product. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as (70 × 12.44 × 5) / 100 = 43.5. FoodEx2 code A0FKM used as closest parent for tea-based beverage with tapioca pearls; no dedicated code exists for milk-free flavoured bubble tea. SNOMED CT code left null — no confirmed code identified. Tapioca pearls are an optional topping and are not included in the base nutritional values reported here.
